title: The String Object 🧵
description: Discover the hidden powers built into every single string.

slides.title: Not Just Dumb Text 🧠
slides.content:
# The Secret Identity

Until now, we've treated strings as simple pieces of dumb text wrapped in quotes.

```javascript
let greeting = "Hello World!";
```

But here is a JavaScript secret: **Everything is an Object!**

The millisecond you create a string, JavaScript secretly wraps it up in a massive 'String Object', overflowing with hidden methods, functions, and properties you can use to interact with it!

slides.title: Wait... Methods? 🤔
slides.content:
## Yes, Dot Notation!

Just like you use Dot Notation (`user.age`) to talk to Objects, or (`highScores.push()`) to talk to Arrays, you can use Dot Notation on literally any string!

```javascript
let message = "LOUD TEXT!";

// We can ask the string to transform itself!
console.log( message.toLowerCase() ); // prints: loud text!
```

You don't have to write your own complex functions to capitalize or lowercase words. JavaScript built them right into the language!

slides.title: Try It Out 💻
slides.content: Use the toLowerCase() and toUpperCase() built-in string methods to easily fix terrible user inputs:

slides.title: Strings are basically Arrays! 🚂
slides.content:
## The Indexing Trick

Remember how arrays organize their cars by indexed numbers (0, 1, 2)?

Strings organize their letters the EXACT same way! A string is essentially just an array of single characters.

```javascript
let word = "APPLE";

console.log( word[0] ); // Prints: 'A'
console.log( word[1] ); // Prints: 'P'
```

Because they act like arrays, we can use simple Bracket Notation to immediately grab the first letter of any word!

quizData.question: What is the output of the following code? let myName = "BOB"; console.log(myName[2]);
quizData.explanation: Just like Arrays, Strings are zero-indexed. B is at 0. O is at 1. The final B is at Index 2!

slides.title: Read Only! 🛑
slides.content:
## Immutable Arrays

While strings *act* like arrays, there is one massive difference: **Strings are Immutable.**

With an array, you can change an item: `arr[0] = 50`. But you CANNOT change a single letter of a string this way!

```javascript
let word = "BOB";
word[0] = "J"; 
console.log(word); // It still prints "BOB"! The edit was ignored!
```

To change a string, you must destroy the entire variable and recreate a brand new string to overwrite it: `word = "JOB";`

quizData.question: Why didn't JavaScript change `word[0]` to 'J' in the previous slide?

options.text: Because you didn't use double quotes
Because JavaScript doesn't support Bracket Notation on strings

quizData.explanation: You can READ string letters using indexes, but you cannot WRITE or alter string letters this way. They are immutable.
